en.extract:
The willow tit (Poecile montanus) is a passerine bird in the tit family, Paridae. It is a widespread and common resident breeder throughout temperate and subarctic Europe and across the Palearctic. The plumage is grey-brown and off-white with a black cap and bib. It is more of a conifer specialist than the closely related marsh tit, which explains its breeding much farther north. It is resident, and most birds do not migrate.


Taxonomy
The willow tit was described in 1827 by the Swiss naturalist Thomas Conrad von Baldenstein under the trinomial name Parus cinereus montanus. The type locality is the mountain forests in the Canton of Grisons, Switzerland. The willow tit is now placed in the genus Poecile that was erected by the German naturalist Johann Jakob Kaup in 1829. The genus name, Poecile, is the Ancient Greek name for a now unidentifiable small bird, and the specific montanus is Latin for "of the mountains".
Poecile was at one time treated as a subgenus within the genus Parus but molecular taxonomic analysis, using both nuclear and mitochondrial genes, supports Poecile as a distinct clade. Within Poecile, most of the Old World species (including the willow tit) form a separate clade from the New World chickadees. The taxonomic analysis has shown that the willow tit is sister to the Caspian tit (Poecile hyrcanus).
There are 14 recognised subspecies:

P. m. kleinschmidti (Hellmayr, 1900) – Britain
P. m. rhenanus (Kleinschmidt, 1900) – northwest France to west Germany, north Switzerland and north Italy
P. m. montanus (Conrad von Baldenstein, 1827) – southeast France to Romania, Bulgaria and Greece
P. m. salicarius (Brehm, CL, 1831) – Germany and west Poland to northeast Switzerland and Austria
P. m. borealis (de Sélys-Longchamps, 1843) – Scandinavia south to Ukraine
P. m. uralensis (Grote, 1927) – southeast European Russia, west Siberia and Kazakhstan
P. m. baicalensis Swinhoe, 1871 – central east, east Siberia, north Mongolia, north China and north Korea
P. m. anadyrensis (Belopolski, 1932) – northeast Siberia
P. m. kamtschatkensis Bonaparte, 1850 – Kamchatka Peninsula and north Kuril Islands
P. m. sachalinensis (Lönnberg, 1908) – Sakhalin and south Kuril Islands
P. m. restrictus (Hellmayr, 1900) – Japan
P. m. songarus (Severtsov, 1873) – southeast Kazakhstan to Kyrgyzstan and northwest China
P. m. affinis Przevalski, 1876 – central north China
P. m. stoetzneri (Kleinschmidt, 1921) – northeast China
The Sichuan tit (Poecile weigoldicus) was formerly treated as a subspecies of the willow tit. It was promoted to species status based on a 2002 phylogenetic analysis that compared DNA sequences from the mitochondrial cytochrome-b gene. The single locus results were later confirmed by a larger multi-locus analysis published in 2017.


Description

The willow tit is 11.5 cm (4.5 in) in length, has a wingspan of 17–20.5 cm (6.7–8.1 in) and weighs around 11 g (0.39 oz). It has a large head, a thin bill, a long dull black cap that descends to the mantle and a black bib. The sides of the face are white, the back is grey-brown and the underparts are buff. The sexes are similar in appearance.
In the east of its range it is much paler than marsh tit, but as one goes west the various races become increasingly similar, so much so that it was not recognised as a breeding bird in Great Britain until the end of the 19th century, despite being widespread.
The willow tit is distinguished from the marsh tit by a sooty brown instead of a glossy blue black cap; the general colour is otherwise similar, though the under parts are more buff and the flanks distinctly more rufous; the pale buff edgings to the secondaries form a light patch on the closed wing. The feathers of the crown and the black bib under the bill are longer, but this is not an easily noticed character.
The commonest call is a nasal zee, zee, zee, but the notes of the bird evidently vary considerably. Occasionally a double note, ipsee, ipsee, is repeated four or five times.


Behaviour and ecology


Breeding

The willow tit excavates its own nesting hole, even piercing hard bark; this is usually in a rotten stump or in a tree, more or less decayed. Most nests are cups of felted material, such as fur, hair and wood chips, but feathers are sometimes used. The eggs are laid daily. The clutch is typically between six and nine eggs. The eggs have a white background and are marked with red-brown speckles and spots which are often concentrated at the broader end. They measure around 15.8 mm × 12.3 mm (0.62 in × 0.48 in) and weigh 1.2 g (0.042 oz). The eggs are incubated by the female alone and hatch after 13–15 days. The chicks are then cared for and fed by both parents but only the female broods the young. The nestlings fledge after 17–20 days. Only a single brood is raised each season.
In a study using ring-recovery data carried out in northern Finland, the survival rate for juveniles for their first year was 0.58, and the subsequent adult annual survival rate was 0.64. For birds that survive the first year the typical lifespan is thus only three years. The maximum recorded age is 11 years; this has been recorded for a bird in Finland and for another near Nottingham in England.


Food and feeding
Birds feed on insects, caterpillars, and seeds, much like other tits. This species is parasitised by the moorhen flea, Dasypsyllus gallinulae.


Status
The willow tit has an extremely large range with an estimated population of between 175 and 253 million mature individuals. This large population appears to be slowly decreasing but the decline is not sufficiently rapid to approach the threshold of vulnerability. The species is therefore classed as of least-concern by the International Union for Conservation of Nature. In contrast, the number in the United Kingdom declined by 83% between 1995 and 2017. There was also a contraction in the range. The rapid decline is believed be due to three factors: habitat loss, competition for nest holes by other tits particularly blue tits, and nest predation by the great spotted woodpecker. Over the same period, the number of great spotted woodpeckers increased fourfold.


References


Sources


External links

Xeno-canto: audio recordings of the willow tit

[[File:Poecile montanus kleinschmidti 2.jpg|thumb|right|In the UK]]

The '''willow tit''' ('''''Poecile montanus''''') is a [[passerine]] [[bird]] in the [[tit (bird)|tit]] family, Paridae. It is a widespread and common resident breeder throughout temperate and subarctic Europe and across the [[Palearctic]]. The plumage is grey-brown and off-white with a black cap and bib. It is more of a [[conifer]] specialist than the closely related [[marsh tit]], which explains its breeding much farther north. It is resident, and most birds do not [[bird migration|migrate]].

==Taxonomy==
The willow tit was [[species description|described]] in 1827 by the Swiss naturalist [[Thomas Conrad von Baldenstein]] under the [[trinomial name]] ''Parus cinereus montanus''.<ref>{{Cite book | last=von Baldenstein | first=Thomas Conrad | author-link=Thomas Conrad von Baldenstein | chapter=Nachrichten über die Sumpf-Meise (Mönchs-Meise) (''Parus palustris'' Linn.) | editor-last=Steinmüller | editor-first=Johann Rudolf | year=1827 | title=Neue Alpina: eine Schrift der Schweizerischen Naturgeschichte Alpen- und Landwirthschaft gewiedmet | volume=2 | language=de | location=Winterthur | publisher=Steiner | pages=30–36 [31] | chapter-url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41754078 }}</ref> The [[type locality (biology)|type locality]] is the mountain forests in the [[Canton of Grisons]], Switzerland.<ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Paynter | editor-first=Raymond A. Jr | year=1986 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=12 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=77 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14482194 }}</ref> The willow tit is now placed in the [[genus]] ''[[Poecile]]'' that was erected by the German naturalist [[Johann Jakob Kaup]] in 1829.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Kaup | first=Johann Jakob  | author-link=Johann Jakob Kaup | year=1829 | title=Skizzirte Entwickelungs-Geschichte und natürliches System der europäischen Thierwelt | volume=c. 1 | language=de | place=Darmstadt | publisher=Carl Wilhelm Leske | page=114 | url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/41576537 }}</ref> The genus name, ''Poecile'', is the [[Ancient Greek]] name for a now unidentifiable small bird, and the specific ''montanus'' is [[Latin]] for "of the mountains".<ref name=job>{{cite book | last= Jobling | first= James A | year= 2010| title= The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | url= https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling | publisher= Christopher Helm | location = London | isbn = 978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling/page/n259 259], 311}}</ref>

''Poecile'' was at one time treated as a subgenus within the genus ''Parus'' but [[molecular phylogenetics|molecular taxonomic]] analysis, using both nuclear and [[mitochondrion|mitochondrial]] genes, supports ''Poecile'' as a distinct clade. Within ''Poecile'', most of the Old World species (including the willow tit) form a separate clade from the New World chickadees.<ref name=johansson>{{Cite journal | last1=Johansson | first1=Ulf S. | last2=Ekman | first2=Jan | last3=Bowie | first3=Rauri C. K. | last4=Halvarsson | first4=Peter | last5=Ohlson | first5=Jan I. | last6=Price | first6=Trevor D. | last7=Ericson | first7=Per G. P. | date=2013 | title=A complete multilocus species phylogeny of the tits and chickadees (Aves: Paridae) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=69 | issue=3 | pages=852–860 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2013.06.019| pmid=23831453 | bibcode=2013MolPE..69..852J }}</ref> The taxonomic analysis has shown that the willow tit is [[sister taxon|sister]] to the [[Caspian tit]] (''Poecile hyrcanus'').<ref name=johansson/><ref name=tritsch>{{Cite journal | last1=Tritsch | first1=Christian | last2=Martens | first2=Jochen | last3=Sun | first3=Yue-Hua | last4=Heim | first4=Wieland | last5=Strutzenberger | first5=Patrick | last6=Päckert | first6=Martin | date=2017 | title=Improved sampling at the subspecies level solves a taxonomic dilemma – A case study of two enigmatic Chinese tit species (Aves, Passeriformes, Paridae, ''Poecile'') | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=107 | pages=538–550 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2016.12.014| pmid=27965081 | bibcode=2017MolPE.107..538T }}</ref>

There are 14 recognised [[subspecies]]:<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | year=2020 | title=Waxwings and their allies, tits & penduline tits  | work=IOC World Bird List Version 10.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/waxwings/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=16 April 2020 }}</ref>

* ''P. m. kleinschmidti'' ([[Carl Eduard Hellmayr|Hellmayr]], 1900) – Britain
* ''P. m. rhenanus'' ([[Otto Kleinschmidt|Kleinschmidt]], 1900) – northwest France to west Germany, north Switzerland and north Italy
* ''P. m. montanus'' ([[Thomas Conrad von Baldenstein|Conrad von Baldenstein]], 1827) – southeast France to Romania, Bulgaria and Greece
* ''P. m. salicarius'' ([[Christian Ludwig Brehm|Brehm, CL]], 1831) – Germany and west Poland to northeast Switzerland and Austria
* ''P. m. borealis'' ([[Edmond de Sélys Longchamps|de Sélys-Longchamps]], 1843) – Scandinavia south to Ukraine
* ''P. m. uralensis'' ([[Hermann Grote|Grote]], 1927) – southeast European Russia, west Siberia and [[Kazakhstan]]
* ''P. m. baicalensis'' [[Robert Swinhoe|Swinhoe]], 1871 – central east, east Siberia, north Mongolia, north China and north Korea
* ''P. m. anadyrensis'' ([[Lev Belopolsky|Belopolski]], 1932) – northeast Siberia
* ''P. m. kamtschatkensis'' [[Charles Lucien Bonaparte|Bonaparte]], 1850 – [[Kamchatka Peninsula]] and north [[Kuril Islands]]
* ''P. m. sachalinensis'' ([[Einar Lönnberg|Lönnberg]], 1908) – [[Sakhalin]] and south [[Kuril Islands]]
* ''P. m. restrictus'' (Hellmayr, 1900) – Japan
* ''[[Songar tit|P. m. songarus]]'' ([[Nikolai Severtzov|Severtsov]], 1873) – southeast [[Kazakhstan]] to [[Kyrgyzstan]] and northwest China
* ''P. m. affinis'' [[Nikolay Przhevalsky|Przevalski]], 1876 – central north China
* ''P. m. stoetzneri'' (Kleinschmidt, 1921) – northeast China

The [[Sichuan tit]] (''Poecile weigoldicus'') was formerly treated as a subspecies of the willow tit. It was promoted to species status based on a 2002 phylogenetic analysis that compared DNA sequences from the [[mitochondrial]] cytochrome-''b'' gene.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Salzburger | first1=Walter | last2=Martens | first2=Jochen | last3=Nazarenko | first3=Alexander A. | last4=Sun | first4=Yua-Hue | last5=Dallinger | first5=Reinhard | last6=Sturmbauer | first6=Christian | date=2002 | title=Phylogeography of the Eurasian Willow Tit (''Parus montanus'') based on DNA sequences of the mitochondrial cytochrome ''b'' gene | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=24 | issue=1 | pages=26–34 | doi=10.1016/S1055-7903(02)00266-X| pmid=12128026 | bibcode=2002MolPE..24...26S }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Eck, S. | last2=Martens, J. | date=2006 | title=Systematic notes on Asian birds. 49. A preliminary review of the Aegithalidae, Remizidae and Paridae | journal=Zoologische Mededelingen | url=http://www.repository.naturalis.nl/record/209958 | volume=80-5 | pages=1–63 [18–19]}}</ref> The single [[Locus (genetics)|locus]] results were later confirmed by a larger multi-locus analysis published in 2017.<ref name=tritsch/>

==Description==
[[File:コガラ Poecile montanus restrictus.JPG|thumb|right|Subspecies ''Poecile montanus restrictus'' in Japan]]
The willow tit is {{cvt|11.5|cm}} in length, has a wingspan of {{cvt|17|-|20.5|cm}} and weighs around {{cvt|11|g}}.{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|pp=169, 184}} It has a large head, a thin bill, a long dull black cap that descends to the {{birdgloss|mantle}} and a black bib. The sides of the face are white, the back is grey-brown and the underparts are buff. The sexes are similar in appearance.{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|p=161}}

In the east of its range it is much paler than marsh tit, but as one goes west the various races become increasingly similar, so much so that it was not recognised as a breeding bird in Great Britain until the end of the 19th century, despite being widespread.

The willow tit is distinguished from the marsh tit by a sooty brown instead of a glossy blue black cap; the general colour is otherwise similar, though the under parts are more buff and the flanks distinctly more [[rufous]]; the pale buff edgings to the secondaries form a light patch on the closed wing. The feathers of the crown and the black bib under the bill are longer, but this is not an easily noticed character.{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|p=161}}

The commonest call is a nasal ''{{Not a typo|zee}}'', ''{{Not a typo|zee}}, {{Not a typo|zee}}'', but the notes of the bird evidently vary considerably. Occasionally a double note, ''{{Not a typo|ipsee}}, {{Not a typo|ipsee}}'', is repeated four or five times.<ref name=hbw>{{ cite book | last1=Gosler | first1=A.G. | last2=Clement | first2=P. | year=2007 | chapter=Family Paridae (Tits and Chickadees) | editor1-last=del Hoyo | editor1-first=J. | editor2-last=Elliott | editor2-first=A. | editor3-last=Sargatal | editor3-first=J. | title=Handbook of the Birds of the World | volume=12: Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees | location=Barcelona, Spain | publisher=Lynx Edicions | isbn=978-84-96553-42-2 | pages=662–750 [712–713] | chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0012unse/page/712/mode/1up | chapter-url-access=registration }}</ref>

==Behaviour and ecology==
===Breeding===
[[File:Poecile montanus MWNH 2284.jpg|thumb|Eggs, collection [[Museum Wiesbaden]], Germany]]
The willow tit excavates its own nesting hole, even piercing hard bark; this is usually in a rotten stump or in a tree, more or less decayed. Most nests are cups of [[felted]] material, such as fur, hair and wood chips, but [[feather]]s are sometimes used. The eggs are laid daily. The clutch is typically between six and nine eggs. The eggs have a white background and are marked with red-brown speckles and spots which are often concentrated at the broader end. They measure around {{cvt|15.8|x|12.3|mm}} and weigh {{cvt|1.2|g}}. The eggs are incubated by the female alone and hatch after 13–15 days. The chicks are then cared for and fed by both parents but only the female broods the young. The nestlings fledge after 17–20 days. Only a single brood is raised each season.{{sfn|Cramp|Perrins|1993|pp=181-182}}

In a study using [[Bird ringing|ring-recovery]] data carried out in northern Finland, the survival rate for juveniles for their first year was 0.58, and the subsequent adult annual survival rate was 0.64.<ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Orell | first1=Markku | last2=Belda | first2=Eduardo J. | date=2002 | title=Delayed cost of reproduction and senescence in the willow tit ''Parus montanus'' | journal=Journal of Animal Ecology | volume=71 | issue=1 | pages=55–64 | doi=10.1046/j.0021-8790.2001.00575.x| doi-access=free | bibcode=2002JAnEc..71...55O }}</ref> For birds that survive the first year the typical lifespan is thus only three years.<ref name=bto>{{cite web | title=Willow Tit ''Poecile montanus'' | work=Bird Facts | date=16 July 2010 | url=https://app.bto.org/birdfacts/results/bob14420.htm | publisher=British Trust for Ornithology | access-date=19 April 2020 | archive-date=3 December 2019 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191203224656/https://app.bto.org/birdfacts/results/bob14420.htm | url-status=dead }}</ref> The maximum recorded age is 11 years; this has been recorded for a bird in Finland and for another near [[Nottingham]] in England.<ref name=euring>{{cite web |title=European Longevity Records: Willow Tit |url=https://euring.org/data-and-codes/longevity-list?page=5 |publisher=Euring: European Union for Bird Ringing |access-date=19 April 2020 |archive-date=18 November 2022 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20221118071252/https://euring.org/data-and-codes/longevity-list?page=5 |url-status=dead }}</ref><ref>{{cite web | title=Ringing and Nest Recording Report: Longevity records for Britain & Ireland in 2018 | publisher=British Trust for Ornithology | location=Thetford | url=https://app.bto.org/ring/countyrec/results2018/longevity.htm#14420 | access-date=20 April 2020 | archive-date=3 December 2019 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191203165339/https://app.bto.org/ring/countyrec/results2018/longevity.htm#14420 | url-status=dead }}</ref>

===Food and feeding===
Birds feed on [[insect]]s, [[caterpillar]]s, and [[Seed|seeds]], much like other tits. This species is parasitised by the [[moorhen flea]], ''Dasypsyllus gallinulae''.<ref name = Rothschild>{{cite book |title= Fleas, Flukes and Cuckoos: A Study of Bird Parasites |author= Rothschild, Miriam |author-link= Miriam Rothschild |author2= Clay, Theresa |year= 1953 |publisher= Collins |location= London |page= 113 |url= https://archive.org/details/fleasflukescucko017900mbp }}</ref>

==Status==
The willow tit has an extremely large range with an estimated population of between 175 and 253 million mature individuals. This large population appears to be slowly decreasing but the decline is not sufficiently rapid to approach the threshold of vulnerability. The species is therefore classed as of [[Least-concern species|least-concern]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]].<ref name="iucn status 19 November 2021" /> In contrast, the number in the United Kingdom declined by 83% between 1995 and 2017. There was also a contraction in the range.<ref name=bbs>{{Cite book | last1=Harris | last2=S.J. | last3=Massimino | last4=D. | last5=Eaton | last6=M.A | last7=Gillings | last8=S. | last9=Noble | last10=D.G | last11=Balmer | last12=D.E | last13=Pearce-Higgins | first13=J.W. | last14=Woodcock | first14=P. | date=2019 | title=The Breeding Bird Survey 2018 | url=https://www.bto.org/sites/default/files/bbs-report-2018.pdf | series=BTO Research Report 717 | publisher=British Trust for Ornithology | location=Thetford | isbn=978-1-912642-05-2}}</ref> The rapid decline is believed be due to three factors: habitat loss, competition for nest holes by other tits particularly blue tits, and nest predation by the [[great spotted woodpecker]].<ref>{{Cite journal | last=Siriwardena | first=Gavin M. | date=2004 | title=Possible roles of habitat, competition and avian nest predation in the decline of the Willow Tit ''Parus montanus'' in Britain | journal=Bird Study | volume=51 | issue=3 | pages=193–202 | doi=10.1080/00063650409461354 | doi-access=free | bibcode=2004BirdS..51..193S }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Lewis|first1=Alex J.G.|last2=Amar|first2=Arjun|last3=Chormonond|first3=Elisabeth C.|last4=Stewort|first4=Finn R.P.|year=2009|title=The decline of the Willow Tit in Britain|url=https://britishbirds.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/article_files/V102/V102_N07/V102_N7_24_31.pdf|journal=British Birds|volume=102|pages=386–393|archive-date=2021-09-02|access-date=2023-02-14|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210902121604/https://britishbirds.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/article_files/V102/V102_N07/V102_N7_24_31.pdf|url-status=dead}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last1=Parry | first1=Wayne | last2=Broughton | first2=Richard K. | date=2018 | title=Nesting behaviour and breeding success of Willow Tits ''Poecile montanus'' in north-west England | journal=Ringing & Migration | volume=33 | issue=2 | pages=75–85 | doi=10.1080/03078698.2018.1631610| s2cid=199097252 | url=http://nora.nerc.ac.uk/id/eprint/524357/1/Nesting%20British%20Willow%20Tits%20Poecile%20montana%20_Parry_and_Broughton_accepted.pdf }}</ref> Over the same period, the number of great spotted woodpeckers increased fourfold.<ref name=bbs/>

zh.extract:
褐头山雀（学名：Poecile montanus）为山雀科高山山雀属的鸟类，俗稱唧唧鬼子。主要分布於整個歐亞大陸的溫帶地區，一般生活于针叶林或针阔混交林。该物种的模式产地在瑞士格勞賓登州的山地森林。


亚种
褐頭山雀東歐亞種（學名：Poecile montanus borealis）：分布於芬諾斯堪底亞、波羅的海沿岸至烏克蘭一帶
褐頭山雀不列顛亞種（學名：Poecile montanus kleinschmidti）：大不列顛島特有亞種
褐頭山雀指名亞種（學名：Poecile montanus montanus）：主要分布於侏羅山脈與東歐地區
褐頭山雀西歐亞種（學名：Poecile montanus rhenanus）：分布於法國西北部至德國西部與義大利北端一帶
褐頭山雀中歐亞種（學名：Poecile montanus salicarius）：分布於德國、波蘭西部至瑞士東北部與奧地利一帶
褐头山雀西北亚种（学名：Poecile montanus affinis），是中国的特有物种。分布于宁夏、甘肃、青海等地。该物种的模式产地在宁夏贺兰山和甘肃。


参考文献
